Document [4] (Title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Etiology) Preexposure prophylaxis is significantly less costly than the treatment of HIV infection, both in terms of duration of use and per dose. The PrEP regimen is prescribed to high-risk individuals and is taken daily until the risk factors diminish, whereas antiretroviral therapy is taken for life by those who acquire HIV. Further, antiretroviral therapy consists of multiple, generally expensive drug combinations versus simpler and less costly options available for PrEP. The WHO defined being at "substantial risk" as anyone with greater than a 3 per 100 person-years risk of acquiring HIV in the absence of PrEP, the level above which PrEP was found to be cost-saving. [41] Below this level, PrEP may still be cost-effective.

Document [10] (Title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Epidemiology -- Demand for and Use of Preexposure Prophylaxis) Racial, ethnic, and other disparities play a role in low PrEP uptake. For example, in the United States in 2019, approximately 40% of persons eligible for PrEP are Black. Yet, the number of White people prescribed PrEP was about 5 times higher than the number of Black people. [52] The CDC estimated that 60.5% of eligible White people, 13.8% of eligible Hispanic/Latino people, and 7.9% of eligible Black people received PrEP. Women use PrEP less than men for the same indications, as do younger people aged 16 to 14 as compared to those older than 25. [52] See StatPearls' companion topic, " HIV Prevention ," for further information on modes of transmission and populations at risk for HIV.

Document [12] (Title: HIV Prevention -- Clinical Significance) Antiretroviral medications used as preexposure prophylaxis (PrEP), postexposure prophylaxis (PEP), or treatment as prevention (TasP) are highly effective in decreasing the transmission of HIV. A cornerstone of any HIV prevention strategy, antiretrovirals should be offered to all at-risk individuals. PrEP and TasP require intensive patient engagement and frequent follow-up visits at a health facility. They must be given in conjunction with access to condoms, regular testing for STIs, and intensive counseling for behavioral risk reduction, including safer sex or injection practices as appropriate. Patients must be advised that antiretrovirals do not prevent other STIs or pregnancy.
Document [13] (Title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Evaluation) HIV testing is the first step in the evaluation of any patient who requests PrEP or may be at risk for HIV. A documented negative HIV test within 7 days before initiating, reinitiating, or continuing PrEP is crucial. This precaution is necessary to mitigate the risk of inducing resistance if HIV is exposed to inadequate retroviral treatment, potentially limiting future treatment options. [20] Patient-reported or documented anonymous results should not be accepted.[CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021]

Document [15] (Title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Introduction) Preexposure prophylaxis of HIV (PrEP) is an essential component of the recommended package for HIV prevention, according to the World Health Organization (WHO) and United States Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). [3] [4] Preexposure prophylaxis (PrEP) involves the intake of antiretroviral medications either continuously or for identified high-risk events and is highly effective in preventing HIV. Compared to placebo, consistent use of daily oral PrEP reduces the risk of sexual transmission of HIV by up to 92%. [5] PrEP with bimonthly injectable cabotegravir lowers the chance of sexual transmission of HIV by about 70% compared to those taking tenofovir disiproxil fumarate/emtricitabine (TDF/FTC). [6] [CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021] Though one trial of PWID observed individuals 40 years or older, those who used methamphetamines, and those with recent incarcerations had lower adherence than those without these risk factors, other studies have indicated the efficacy and cost-effectiveness of PrEP in preventing HIV in this population. [7] [8]
Document [16] (Title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Treatment / Management -- Eligibility) Antiretroviral medications used as PrEP are highly effective in decreasing HIV acquisition. These medications should be offered based on clinical judgment to all high-risk individuals as defined by local, state, or country guidelines. While the WHO highlights the need for PrEP according to key populations, individual countries or other authorities develop guidelines based on country or local epidemiology, and clinicians make decisions based on local epidemiology and individual risk factors.

Document [23] (Title: Emtricitabine -- Indications) The FDA-approved, combined drug, containing emtricitabine and tenofovir is used as pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) to prevent HIV infection. [5] [6] Indications for PrEP use include someone who has sex with an HIV-positive individual, having sex with individuals of unknown HIV status and not being in a monogamous relationship, having a past sexually transmitted infection within the prior six months, using condoms inconsistently in the past, and exchanging sex for money or other goods. [7] PrEP is also indicated for patients using intravenous drugs or entering into a drug rehabilitation program within the last six months. [7] The PROUD study tested the effectiveness of PrEP in HIV-negative men in clinics across England in 2013. [8] The PROUD study reduced the incidence of HIV by 86% in the PrEP group in a patient population where the rate of HIV was seven times greater than the population of men who have sex with men in the same clinics gathered from 2012. [8]

Seeking further financial support may improve adherence for those who do not have insurance and cannot afford the medications. For patients with substance use disorders, referral for counseling or a treatment program may be successful. Options should be tailored to fit the client's preferences and life situation. All measures must be supportive, recognizing the challenges in maintaining long-term adherence to therapy and the socioeconomic context in which the client lives.

Document [27] (Title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Pearls and Other Issues) Key facts to keep in mind regarding PrEP include the following: PrEP involves the consistent use of antiretroviral drugs by individuals who are HIV-negative and at high risk of acquisition to protect themselves from the infection. PrEP greatly reduces the risk of HIV infection if consistently taken as prescribed. The effectiveness of PrEP drops significantly if the individual has poor adherence to medication use. PrEP is cost-effective and even cost-saving when implemented in populations at high risk for HIV infection. PrEP offers no protection against other sexually transmitted infections or pregnancy. PrEP for the prevention of HIV must be delivered as part of a comprehensive package that counsels and supports the patient to prevent sexually transmitted infections, manage adverse effects, and maintain adherence grounded in the patient's socioeconomic reality.
Document [28] (Title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Complications -- HIV Acquisition) Acquiring HIV infection while being prescribed PrEP mainly occurs due to nonadherence to medication by stopping, infrequently using, or stopping and starting treatment. [84] Socioeconomic disadvantage and a lack of awareness of the risks may contribute to nonadherence. Rarely, infection can occur despite high adherence because of exposure to a drug-resistant strain or simply because PrEP protection is not 100%.[CDC. PrEP Supplement. 2021]
Document [29] (Title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Introduction) In 2015, the WHO first recommended PrEP to anyone who was at substantial risk for HIV infection as part of combination HIV prevention programs. Populations that may benefit from preexposure chemoprophylaxis are those with an HIV incidence rate of about 3 per 100 person-years or higher.[WHO. Consolidated HIV Guidelines. 2021] The addition of PrEP to locally available prevention measures is necessary to end global transmission of HIV, particularly in areas with a high burden of HIV. [9] However, PrEP remains underutilized. In 2022, more than 2.5 million people worldwide received PrEP at least once, far short of the 10 million person target, but a considerable advance from 2019 when only 233,00 people were estimated to have received PrEP.[UNAIDS. Global Report. 2023]
Document [30] (Title: Preexposure Prophylaxis for HIV Prevention -- Prognosis) While HIV is not curable, people who are infected can live long, healthy lives due to the advent of highly active antiretroviral therapies. The prognosis for individuals seeking prevention of HIV infection is excellent if treatment adherence can be maintained. The effectiveness of oral PrEP in preventing HIV acquisition during sexual contact is approximately 99% when medication is strictly adhered to; effectiveness is significantly decreased if adherence is not maintained.[CDC. PrEP Guidelines. 2021] Pooled analyses of results from large trials of TDF/FTC versus placebo showed a relative risk of HIV acquisition of 0.46 after 4 months to 4 years of follow-up. [68] After categorizing study outcomes based on PrEP adherence levels, the relative risk was 0.27 for studies with adherence of 70% or higher, 0.51 for studies with adherence between 40% and less than 70%, and 0.93 (95% CI .72-1.2). [52]
Document [31] (Title: HIV Prophylaxis (Archived) -- Introduction) HIV prophylaxis is divided into 2 categories: post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) and pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP). PEP is the administration of antiretroviral therapy to an HIV-negative person who may be exposed to HIV. Exposure can be occupational (oPEP) or non-occupational (nPEP), with non-occupational exposure being related to sexual or intravenous (IV) drug exposures. PrEP is the use of antiretroviral drugs in a high-risk, HIV-negative patient to prevent future HIV infections.

Document [3] (Title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 65) HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. A variety of antiretroviral agents are recognized for their ability to prevent acquisition of HIV, including the non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) delavirdine, delavirdine‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine, and doravirine, as well as the integrase inhibitors dolutegravir, raltegravir, and cabotegravir. Among these, cabotegravir is also noted for its therapeutic role in treating established HIV infection. Delavirdine, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine, and doravirine are all members of the class “Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors,” while dolutegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir belong to the class “Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use.” Doravirine carries a specific contraindication with the antimicrobial rifapentine, indicating that co‑administration should be avoided. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how both NNRTIs and integrase inhibitors contribute to HIV prevention strategies—such as pre‑exposure prophylaxis—and, in the case of cabotegravir, to ongoing treatment regimens, underscoring the importance of class‑based drug selection and awareness of drug‑specific contraindications in comprehensive patient care.

Document [6] (Title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 135) HIV infection is strongly associated with shared needle use, which constitutes a major transmission route, and its prevention and treatment rely on antiretroviral agents classified by mechanism of action. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, mimic natural nucleosides, and cause chain termination, thereby inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Key NRTIs include tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, didanosine (dideoxycytidine), emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, and zidovudine; each has demonstrated activity in pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and in early or combination treatment regimens to suppress viral replication. The non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor delavirdine mesylate also possesses preventive properties by directly inhibiting reverse transcriptase. Integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir—block the viral integrase enzyme, preventing integration of viral DNA into the host genome; cabotegravir is notable for its dual use in both treatment of established infection and PrEP, while the other agents are primarily employed for treatment but may be used preventively in high‑risk settings. Collectively, the correct classification and combined use of NRTIs, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, and integrase inhibitors form the backbone of modern HIV prevention strategies and therapeutic protocols, optimizing patient outcomes through viral suppression and reduced acquisition risk.
Document [7] (Title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 73) Direct‑acting antivirals (DAAs) encompass several pharmacologic classes used to treat viral infections, including HIV; within DAAs, cyclic amines such as rimantadin alter cellular synthetic activity, while integrase inhibitors, non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) and nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) constitute additional subclasses. NRTIs include abacavir (synonymous with dideoxyadenosine), dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine, and tenofovir disoproxil, and are employed both for HIV prevention (e.g., abacavir, emtricitabine, lamivudine) and therapy. NNRTIs comprise delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, doravirine, rilpivirine, and raltegravir‑type agents that bind distinct sites on reverse transcriptase; doravirine is contraindicated with rifampicin and rilpivirine with pantoprazole due to drug‑drug interaction risks. Integrase inhibitors—dolutegravir, elvitegravir, raltegravir, cabotegravir, and fostemsavir—block the viral integrase enzyme required for proviral integration. Fixed‑dose combination regimens such as abacavir/dolutegravir/lamivudine and abacavir/lamivudine (also representing dideoxyadenosine/lamivudine) are classified as “Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations” and exemplify DAA‑based HIV therapy. Antiviral combinations specifically formulated for HIV are themselves a type of DAA. Shared needle use is identified as a direct risk factor for HIV acquisition, whereas methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, underscoring important safety considerations in this population. Together, these entities delineate the classification, mechanisms of action, therapeutic and preventive roles, combination formulations, nomenclature equivalences, and contraindications relevant to comprehensive HIV management.

Document [12] (Title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 105) HIV infection is primarily transmitted through shared needle use, and its prevention and treatment rely on a broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents that target the viral reverse transcriptase enzyme. Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) – including tenofovir disoproxil, emtricitabine (also referenced as emtricitabin), lamivudine (lamivudin), stavudine (stavudin), zidovudine (zidovudin), and dideoxycytidine – act as nucleoside analogues that are incorporated into viral DNA and cause premature chain termination, forming a core component of combination HIV therapy. Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) – doravirine, delavirdine (and its mesylate formulation), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine – bind to a distinct site on reverse transcriptase to inhibit viral replication. Doravirine has a specific contraindication when co‑administered with the rifamycin antibiotic rifapentine, reflecting a clinically important drug‑class interaction. Understanding the class membership, mechanisms of action, preventive potential, and contraindications of these NRTI and NNRTI agents is essential for optimizing patient care, selecting effective regimens, managing drug interactions, and reducing the spread of HIV.

The HIV‑combination subclass includes fixed‑dose regimens abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and abacavir / lamivudine, both belonging to the larger HIV‑combination antiviral group. Additional antiretrovirals for HIV infection comprise the entry inhibitor enfuvirtide, the fusion‑inhibitor ibalizumab, nucleoside and nucleotide reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) such as abacavir, tenofovir disoproxil, lamivudine, emtricitabine, stavudine, zidovudine, and dideoxycytidine, non‑nucleoside reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) including delavirdine (and delavirdine‑mesylate), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine (the latter contraindicated with pantoprazole), and
Document [16] (Title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 62) the importance of recognizing contraindicated drug classes, and the role of antiviral agents such as zanamivir in preventing viral respiratory illnesses that can lead to secondary bacterial or fungal pneumonias.
Document [17] (Title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1216) Nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors constitute a class of antiretroviral agents that target the HIV reverse transcriptase enzyme, thereby blocking viral replication. Tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, and stavudine are all members of this nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitor class. By incorporating these drugs into combination antiretroviral therapy, clinicians can achieve more effective suppression of HIV, improve patient outcomes, and reduce the risk of resistance development.

Document [19] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1230) Antivirals for treatment of HIV infections, combinations comprise specific antiretroviral regimens, including the three‑drug combination abacavir / dolutegravir / lamivudine and the two‑drug combination abacavir / lamivudine. Both regimens are classified as members of the broader category of combination antiviral therapies used to manage HIV infection.
Document [20] (Title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 0 -- Community 18) illustrate how antiviral, antiretroviral, and antifungal drugs are organized into mechanistic ATC classes—direct‑acting antivirals, other antivirals, entry inhibitors, integrase inhibitors, NNRTIs, NRTIs, non‑RT nucleoside analogues, neuraminidase inhibitors, and echinocandins—and how specific agents within each class target distinct viral or fungal processes. The relationships also link drugs to preventive or therapeutic indications (HIV prevention, HIV treatment, hepatitis C, hepatitis B/D, influenza, smallpox, viral infection prophylaxis) and to epidemiologic contexts such as HIV infection (a viral infection associated with shared needle use) and influenza‑related pneumonia, thereby supporting informed therapeutic decision‑making and public‑health control.
Document [21] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1178) HIV infection is a viral disease that is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, a major route of transmission among individuals who inject drugs. Because of the potential for adverse effects and drug interactions, methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, meaning it should not be used in this population. Conversely, the antiretroviral agent delavirdine mesylate has been noted to possess preventive activity against HIV infection, offering a possible strategy for reducing the risk of acquiring the virus. Understanding these relationships—transmission through shared needles, the contraindication of methotrexate sodium, and the preventive potential of delavirdine mesylate—is essential for optimizing patient care and guiding therapeutic decisions in the context of HIV.

Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 512) HIV infection is closely linked to behaviors such as shared needle use, which facilitates viral transmission. The use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, highlighting the need for careful medication selection in this population. Several antiretroviral agents are employed to prevent or treat HIV infection. Delavirdine‑mesylate, a non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor, may be used for prophylaxis against HIV infection. Integrase inhibitors—including cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir—belong to the same pharmacologic class of systematic anti‑infectives and are effective in both preventing viral integration and treating established HIV infection. Cabotegravir is specifically noted for its therapeutic role, while dolutegravir, elvitegravir, and raltegravir are recognized for their preventive potential. Understanding these relationships assists clinicians in selecting appropriate prophylactic and therapeutic strategies while avoiding contraindicated drugs such as methotrexate sodium in individuals living with HIV.
Document [24] (Title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 113) HIV infection is closely linked to the behavioral risk factor of shared needle use, underscoring the importance of harm‑reduction counseling in prevention strategies. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents demonstrates preventive activity against HIV acquisition, including delavirdin, delavirdin‑mesylate, dolutegravir, efavirenz, elvitegravir, emtricitabine, etravirine, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil. Cabotegravir is recognized for therapeutic use in established HIV infection. These drugs are organized into pharmacologic classes that reflect distinct mechanisms of action: non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) comprise doravirine, delavirdin, efavirenz, etravirine, rilpivirine and nevirapine, which bind to a specific site on HIV reverse transcriptase; nucleoside and nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) include dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine and tenofovir disoproxil, acting as intracellularly phosphorylated chain terminators; and integrase inhibitors—cabotegravir, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir—target viral DNA integration. Notable drug‑drug contraindications are identified within these classes: doravirine is contraindicated with rifapentine, and rilpivirine is contraindicated with pantoprazole, highlighting the need for careful medication reconciliation. Recognizing the relationships among needle‑sharing risk, the preventive and therapeutic roles of specific antiretrovirals, their class‑based mechanisms, and relevant contraindications is essential for constructing safe, effective HIV prophylaxis and treatment regimens and for optimizing patient counseling and care.
Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1828) Acyclovir 50 mg/mL topical cream is classified as a topical antiviral medication, belonging to the broader category of Antivirals, topical.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1606) HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, a major route of transmission. In the context of HIV prevention and therapy, delavirdin‑mesylate and dolutegravir are identified as agents that may help prevent acquisition of the virus, while fostemsavir is recognized for its potential to treat established HIV infection. Conversely, methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in individuals with HIV infection, indicating that this medication should be avoided in this patient population. Understanding these relationships supports informed decision‑making in both preventive strategies and therapeutic management of HIV‑related care.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 650) HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which represents a major route of transmission. The use of methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, highlighting the need for careful medication selection in this population. Several antiretroviral agents are associated with the prevention or treatment of HIV infection. Delavirdin‑mesylate and dolutegravir have been reported to have preventive activity against HIV acquisition, and raltegravir is also recognized for its ability to prevent HIV infection. Cabotegravir, in contrast, is used primarily for the treatment of established HIV infection. Dolutegravir, cabotegravir, and raltegravir all belong to the class of integrase inhibitors, a group of systematic anti‑infective agents that target the viral integrase enzyme to block viral replication. Understanding these relationships assists clinicians in selecting appropriate preventive strategies, therapeutic regimens, and avoiding contraindicated drugs for individuals living with or at risk for HIV.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 234) HIV infection is closely linked to the practice of shared needle use, which serves as a major transmission route. The immunosuppressive agent methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, reflecting the need to avoid further compromise of immune function. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral medications is employed to prevent or treat HIV infection. Among the integrase inhibitors, cabotegravir, dolutegravir, and raltegravir are classified as members of the “Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use” group; cabotegravir is specifically noted for its therapeutic use in HIV infection, while dolutegravir and raltegravir are recognized for their preventive potential. The non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) class includes delavirdine (and its mesylate form), efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, and rilpivirine, all of which are members of the “Non‑nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors” group and are capable of preventing HIV infection. Additionally, pantoprazole is identified as having a contraindicated class relationship with rilpivirine, indicating that co‑administration may be unsafe. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of selecting appropriate antiretroviral agents, recognizing drug‑specific contraindications, and addressing behavioral risk factors such as needle sharing in the comprehensive management of HIV infection.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1649) Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) constitute a class of antiretroviral drugs that inhibit HIV reverse transcriptase by binding to a distinct site on the enzyme, causing conformational changes that block viral DNA synthesis. Both efavirenz and etravirine are identified as members of the Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors class, and nevirapin is also recognized as a member of this same class. Recognizing that efavirenz, etravirine, and nevirapin all belong to the Non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors group aids clinicians in selecting appropriate HIV treatment regimens and in anticipating class‑related efficacy and adverse‑effect profiles.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 54) Enfuvirtid, delavirdin‑mesilát, dolutegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are antiretroviral agents that may prevent HIV infection, indicating a role in pre‑exposure prophylaxis. Cabotegravir and fostemsavir are antiretroviral drugs that may be used to treat HIV infection, providing therapeutic options for individuals with established disease. Dolutegravir, elvitegravir, cabotegravir and raltegravir are classified as members of the Integrase Inhibitors, anti‑infectives for systematic use, reflecting their common mechanism of inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme. HIV infection is associated with the shared use of needles, underscoring the importance of harm‑reduction strategies to prevent transmission. Together, these relationships highlight both preventive and treatment modalities within the integrase inhibitor class and emphasize a key behavioral risk factor for HIV acquisition.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2032) Cabotegravir, elvitegravir and raltegravir are all members of the pharmacologic class identified as Integrase Inhibitors, antiinfectives for systematic use. As integrase inhibitors, each of these agents targets the HIV integrase enzyme, thereby preventing the integration of viral DNA into the host genome and serving as essential components of systemic antiretroviral therapy. Their shared classification underscores a common mechanism of action and therapeutic purpose in the treatment of HIV infection, highlighting the importance of this drug class in modern HIV management.
